scenario.name: Operation Dark Harvest
scenario.description: An external attacker compromises a vulnerable web application (DVWA) on web-srv, escalates privileges, and exfiltrates database contents. Investigate the full kill chain using Wazuh alerts, FIM events, and web server logs.
kill_chain_summary: Web shell upload via file upload vulnerability

challenges.name: [Pup] High-Severity Alert Count
challenges.description:
How many alerts with rule.level >= 10 (High severity) were generated on the day of the attack for agent web-srv?

Use the Wazuh Dashboard to filter alerts by agent name and severity level for the attack date (2026-03-01).

hints.content: In Wazuh Dashboard, navigate to Security Events. Use the filter bar to add: agent.name: web-srv AND rule.level >= 10
Set the time range to 2026-03-01 00:00:00 UTC through 2026-03-01 23:59:59 UTC. The total hits count at the top of the results is your answer.
The answer is a three-digit number between 200 and 300.

challenges.name: [Pup] Top Attacker IP Address
challenges.description:
What source IP address generated the most web attack alerts against web-srv?

Identify the attacker's IP by looking at which external address produced the highest number of alerts in the web attack rule group.

hints.content: Look at the data.srcip field in the Wazuh alerts. You can create a visualization or use a Top N aggregation on this field.
Filter by rule.groups: web AND agent.name: web-srv first, then check which source IP has the highest document count.
The IP address is in the 198.51.100.0/24 range (RFC 5737 documentation range).

challenges.name: [Pup] First SQLi Detection Rule
challenges.description:
What Wazuh rule ID (rule.id) detected the first SQL Injection attempt against web-srv?

Find the earliest alert that corresponds to a SQL Injection detection rule in the web-srv logs.

hints.content: Search for alerts containing 'SQL injection' in the rule.description field. Sort by timestamp ascending to find the first one.
The rule belongs to the 31100-31110 range which covers web attack detections in Wazuh's default ruleset.
Rule 31103 is the Wazuh default rule for 'SQL injection attempt' detected in web server access logs.

challenges.name: [Pup] Nikto Scan Alert Count
challenges.description:
How many alerts were generated by the Nikto web vulnerability scanner during the reconnaissance phase against web-srv?

Filter alerts by the scanning tool's User-Agent string or by the reconnaissance rule group to count total Nikto-related alerts.

hints.content: Search for alerts where the full_log or data.http_user_agent field contains 'Nikto' on the web-srv agent.
You can also filter by the reconnaissance rule group or search for alerts from the attacker IP 198.51.100.23 during the early morning hours of the attack.
The answer is a three-digit number between 150 and 200.

challenges.name: [Pup] Web Log Source Location
challenges.description:
What is the full file path of the log file on web-srv that captured the web attack traffic?

Identify the location field in the Wazuh alerts that indicates which log file the web server events were read from.

hints.content: In the Wazuh alert documents, look at the 'location' field. This tells you the source file path that the Wazuh agent was monitoring when it captured the event.
Filter by agent.name: web-srv and rule.groups: web. Check the 'location' field of any matching alert -- it will show the Apache access log path.
Apache on Debian/Ubuntu stores its access log under /var/log/apache2/. The filename is access.log.

challenges.name: [Pup] SQL Injection Alert Total
challenges.description:
How many total SQL Injection alerts were generated across the entire attack against web-srv?

Count all alerts where the rule description or rule group indicates a SQL Injection attempt.

hints.content: Search for alerts where rule.description contains 'SQL injection' or where rule.id is 31103 on agent web-srv.
Make sure your time range covers the full attack window (2026-03-01). The total hits count at the top of the results is your answer.
The answer is a three-digit number between 180 and 200.

challenges.name: [Pup] FIM New Files Detected
challenges.description:
How many new files were detected by Wazuh's File Integrity Monitoring (FIM / syscheck) as 'added' events on web-srv during the attack?

Filter syscheck alerts for events where a new file was created (event type: added).

hints.content: Look for syscheck alerts on web-srv where syscheck.event is 'added'. These indicate new files that were not present in the previous FIM baseline.
Filter by rule.groups: syscheck AND agent.name: web-srv. Then narrow down to events where the syscheck event type indicates a new file was added.
The answer is a single-digit number. The attacker created very few new files to minimize detection footprint.

challenges.name: [Pup] Reconnaissance Scanner Name
challenges.description:
What is the name of the web vulnerability scanning tool the attacker used during the reconnaissance phase?

Provide just the tool name (not the version number).

hints.content: Check the User-Agent strings in web attack alerts from the attacker IP. The scanning tool name appears at the beginning of the User-Agent string.
This is one of the most popular open-source web server scanners. It tests web servers for dangerous files, outdated server software, and other problems.
The tool name starts with 'N' and has five letters total.

challenges.name: [Pup] Maximum Rule Severity Level
challenges.description:
What is the highest rule.level value found in all alerts for web-srv on the day of the attack?

Wazuh rule levels range from 0 to 15. Find the maximum severity level that was triggered.

hints.content: In Wazuh Dashboard, filter by agent.name: web-srv. Then sort by rule.level descending, or create a Max aggregation on the rule.level field.
Wazuh rule levels go from 0 (ignored) to 15 (maximum severity). Custom correlation rules often have the highest severity levels.
The answer is a two-digit number representing the maximum possible severity in Wazuh's rule classification.

challenges.name: [Pup] Target Agent Name
challenges.description:
What is the agent.name of the Wazuh agent installed on the compromised web server in this scenario?

Identify the Wazuh agent name that reported all the alerts related to this attack.

hints.content: Look at the agent.name field in any alert from Scenario 1. All alerts for this scenario come from a single agent.
The agent name describes the server role. In this scenario the target is the web server.
The agent name follows the pattern of the server function with a '-srv' suffix.

challenges.name: [Hunter] Reconnaissance User-Agent
challenges.description:
What User-Agent string did the attacker use during the reconnaissance phase against web-srv?

The attacker used a well-known scanning tool before exploiting the web application. Identify the User-Agent from the HTTP request logs captured by Wazuh.

hints.content: Look at the data.http_user_agent or data.win.eventdata.userAgent fields in alerts from the attacker's source IP during the early phase of the attack.
The scanning tool is an open-source web server scanner. Its User-Agent contains the tool name and version number separated by a forward slash.
Nikto is a popular web vulnerability scanner. Check the version string format: ToolName/X.Y.Z

challenges.name: [Hunter] Uploaded Web Shell Filename
challenges.description:
What is the filename (including extension) of the web shell that the attacker uploaded to web-srv?

After exploiting the SQL injection vulnerability, the attacker uploaded a web shell. Use Wazuh's File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) alerts to identify the malicious file.

hints.content: Look for FIM alerts (rule.groups: syscheck) on the web-srv agent. The web shell was placed in a web-accessible directory.
Check the syscheck.path field for new files created under /var/www/html/ or subdirectories like /var/www/html/dvwa/hackable/uploads/.
The web shell is a PHP file with a very short, common name that suggests command execution capability.

challenges.name: [Hunter] Post-Escalation Command
challenges.description:
After escalating privileges on web-srv, what command did the attacker execute to verify their elevated access?

Correlate sudo/privilege escalation alerts with subsequent command execution events to identify the verification command.

hints.content: After a privilege escalation, attackers typically run a command to confirm they now have root/elevated access. Look for audit or command-monitoring alerts immediately after the escalation event.
Search for rule.id 5715 (sudo-related) or audit events from web-srv and examine commands executed within seconds after the privilege escalation alert.
The command is a standard Linux utility that displays user identity information (UID, GID, groups).

challenges.name: [Hunter] SQLi Exploitation User-Agent
challenges.description:
After the initial Nikto reconnaissance, the attacker switched to a specialized SQL injection exploitation tool. What is the User-Agent string (including version) used during the SQL injection exploitation phase?

Examine the HTTP User-Agent headers in the SQL injection alerts to identify the automated exploitation tool.

hints.content: Look at the User-Agent field in the SQL injection alerts (rule.id: 31103). The exploitation tool has a different User-Agent than the Nikto scanner used during recon.
The tool is the most popular open-source SQL injection exploitation framework. Its name starts with 'sql' and the User-Agent follows the format toolname/version.
sqlmap is the industry-standard SQL injection tool. The version format is sqlmap/X.Y.Z.

challenges.name: [Hunter] Web Shell Full Path
challenges.description:
What is the full absolute file system path of the web shell that the attacker uploaded to web-srv?

Use the FIM (syscheck) alerts to find the complete path where the malicious PHP file was placed on disk.

hints.content: Look at syscheck alerts on web-srv for newly added files. The syscheck.path field contains the full absolute path of each monitored file.
The web shell was uploaded through the DVWA file upload functionality. DVWA stores uploaded files in a specific 'uploads' directory within its web root.
The DVWA upload directory is typically at /var/www/html/dvwa/hackable/uploads/. Look for a .php file in that location.

challenges.name: [Hunter] Sudo Command Count
challenges.description:
How many sudo-related alerts were triggered on web-srv during the attack?

Count all alerts associated with sudo usage, including both successful and failed attempts, to understand the scope of the privilege escalation activity.

hints.content: Search for alerts where full_log contains 'sudo' or where rule.id matches sudo-related rules (5400-5410 range) on agent web-srv.
You can also filter by rule.groups containing 'sudo' or 'pam' to capture all privilege-related events. Make sure your time range covers the full attack day.
The answer is a two-digit number between 20 and 30.

challenges.name: [Hunter] Cron Persistence File Path
challenges.description:
The attacker established persistence by creating a scheduled cron job on web-srv. What is the full path of the crontab file that was modified?

Examine FIM alerts and cron-related events to identify the exact file the attacker used for persistence.

hints.content: Look for syscheck or cron-related alerts (rule.id: 87934 or rule.groups containing 'cron') on web-srv. The attacker modified a per-user crontab file.
On Linux systems, per-user crontab files are stored in /var/spool/cron/crontabs/. Since the attacker had root access, they modified the root user's crontab.
The full path follows the pattern /var/spool/cron/crontabs/<username>. The attacker had root privileges.

challenges.name: [Hunter] Data Staging Command
challenges.description:
Before exfiltrating the stolen database dump, the attacker compressed and staged the data. What is the exact tar command used to create the staging archive?

Search command execution or audit alerts for tar-related activity on web-srv after the database dump.

hints.content: Look for command execution alerts containing 'tar' on web-srv. The attacker compressed the database dump before exfiltration to reduce transfer size.
The attacker used a hidden directory under /tmp/ for staging. Look for tar commands that reference paths containing '.cache' in the audit or command monitoring logs.
The format is: tar czf <output_archive> <input_file>. The archive was created from the SQL dump file stored in /tmp/.cache/.

challenges.name: [Hunter] Exfiltration Destination Port
challenges.description:
What TCP port did the attacker use for data exfiltration from web-srv to the external command-and-control server?

Analyze network connection alerts or outbound traffic events to identify the non-standard port used for data transfer.

hints.content: Look for network connection alerts or curl commands in the audit logs from web-srv that show outbound connections to the attacker's IP (198.51.100.23) on an unusual port.
The exfiltration port is a well-known port commonly associated with reverse shells and Metasploit. It is a four-digit number where all digits are the same.
Port 4444 is the default Metasploit listener port and is frequently used for C2 communications and data exfiltration.

challenges.name: [Hunter] HTTP POST Request Count
challenges.description:
How many HTTP POST requests from the attacker IP were logged in the web server alerts on web-srv?

POST requests often indicate form submissions, file uploads, or exploitation attempts. Count all POST requests from the attacker's source IP.

hints.content: Search the web server alerts for HTTP method POST. Filter by data.srcip: 198.51.100.23 and look for 'POST' in the full_log field on agent web-srv.
In Apache access logs, the HTTP method appears right after the source IP and timestamp. You can search for '"POST ' (with the quote and space) in full_log to match the log format.
The answer is a two-digit number between 40 and 60.

challenges.name: [Alpha] MITRE Persistence Technique ID
challenges.description:
What is the MITRE ATT&CK Technique ID associated with the persistence mechanism the attacker established on web-srv?

The attacker set up a persistence mechanism to maintain access. Identify the specific MITRE ATT&CK technique ID by analyzing the post-exploitation alerts and correlating with the ATT&CK framework.

hints.content: The attacker created a scheduled task for persistence. Look for alerts with rule.mitre.id populated that relate to task scheduling on Linux.
In the MITRE ATT&CK framework, T1053 is 'Scheduled Task/Job'. The sub-technique for cron on Linux is T1053.003 (Cron).
Filter by rule.mitre.technique: 'Scheduled Task/Job' and check the syscheck alerts for modifications to /etc/crontab or files in /etc/cron.d/.

challenges.name: [Alpha] Custom Correlation Rule
challenges.description:
The custom Wazuh rule that correlates the SQLi-to-webshell-to-command-execution attack chain (within a 5-minute window) has a specific rule ID. What is the rule ID of this custom correlation rule?

Examine the local_rules.xml file or search for high-level custom rules (rule.id >= 87900) that triggered during the attack.

hints.content: Custom correlation rules in WazuhBOTS are numbered starting at 87900. Search for alerts where rule.id >= 87900 on the web-srv agent.
The correlation rule fires when it detects a sequence: SQL injection alert (rule 31103) followed by FIM new-file alert followed by command execution alert, all within a frequency window.
Look for rule.id 87901 in the alerts. Its description references 'Web attack chain detected' or similar correlation language.

challenges.name: [Alpha] Exfiltrated Database Table
challenges.description:
What database table was exfiltrated from the MySQL instance on web-srv? Provide the exact mysqldump command the attacker used.

Correlate command execution logs with network egress alerts to identify the data exfiltration activity.

hints.content: Look for command audit events (auditd or Wazuh command monitoring) that reference mysqldump or mysql on web-srv. The attacker targeted a specific table.
The attacker dumped a table from the DVWA database. The table contains user credentials. Check the data.audit.execve fields or full_log for the complete command.
The command follows the format: mysqldump -u <user> <database> <table>. The target database is 'dvwa' and the table contains user account data.

challenges.name: [Alpha] Reconnaissance Start Timestamp
challenges.description:
What is the exact UTC timestamp of the first reconnaissance event from the attacker against web-srv?

Identify the precise moment the attack began by finding the earliest alert generated by the attacker's scanning activity. Provide the full ISO 8601 timestamp.

hints.content: Filter alerts by data.srcip: 198.51.100.23 AND agent.name: web-srv. Sort by @timestamp ascending to find the very first alert from the attacker.
The first event is a Nikto scan request. The reconnaissance started in the early morning hours UTC on 2026-03-01. Look for the first alert between 08:00 and 09:00 UTC.
The timestamp format is ISO 8601: YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ. The recon started at 08:14 UTC.

challenges.name: [Alpha] SQLi MITRE Technique ID
challenges.description:
What MITRE ATT&CK Technique ID is mapped to the SQL Injection alerts in this attack against web-srv?

Examine the rule.mitre.id field in the SQL injection detection alerts to identify the corresponding ATT&CK technique.

hints.content: Look at the rule.mitre.id field in any SQL injection alert (rule.id: 31103) on web-srv. The MITRE technique describes how the attacker gained initial access.
SQL Injection is a method to exploit public-facing applications. In the MITRE ATT&CK framework, this falls under the Initial Access tactic.
T1190 is 'Exploit Public-Facing Application' in the MITRE ATT&CK framework, which covers web application attacks including SQL injection.

challenges.name: [Alpha] Correlation Chain Max Rule ID
challenges.description:
The WazuhBOTS platform includes custom correlation rules that chain multiple attack stages together. What is the rule ID of the highest-level correlation rule that fired during the Dark Harvest attack?

This rule represents the most complete detection of the full attack chain and has the maximum severity level.

hints.content: Search for alerts with rule.id >= 88000 on web-srv. The correlation rules in the 88000+ range represent cross-phase attack chain detections with the highest severity levels.
The correlation rules form a hierarchy: 88000, 88001, 88002. The highest rule ID represents the most complete detection covering the full kill chain from exploitation through exfiltration.

Rule 88002 is the full-chain correlation rule that fires when exploitation, privilege escalation, and data exfiltration are all detected on the same host within a time window. It has severity level 15.

challenges.name: [Alpha] Web Shell MD5 Hash
challenges.description:
What is the MD5 hash of the web shell file uploaded to web-srv, as reported by Wazuh's File Integrity Monitoring?

FIM alerts include cryptographic hashes of monitored files. Find the MD5 hash value for the web shell file in the syscheck alert data.

hints.content: Look at the FIM (syscheck) alert for the web shell file (cmd.php). The syscheck.md5_after field contains the MD5 hash of the file after it was created.
Filter by rule.groups: syscheck AND syscheck.path: *cmd.php on web-srv. Examine the hash fields in the alert document.
The MD5 hash is a 32-character hexadecimal string. It starts with 'd41d8c' -- this is a well-known hash value.

challenges.name: [Alpha] Distinct MITRE Techniques Count
challenges.description:
How many distinct MITRE ATT&CK technique IDs (including sub-techniques) appear across all alerts for the Dark Harvest scenario?

Aggregate the unique values in the rule.mitre.id field across all web-srv alerts to count the total number of distinct techniques observed.

hints.content: In the Wazuh Dashboard, create a Terms aggregation on rule.mitre.id for agent web-srv. Count the number of unique technique IDs that appear.

The attack chain spans multiple MITRE ATT&CK tactics from Reconnaissance through Exfiltration. Each phase maps to one or more technique IDs. Remember to count sub-techniques (e.g., T1053.003) as distinct from parent techniques.

The answer is a single-digit number. The techniques include: T1595.002 (Active Scanning), T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application), T1505.003 (Web Shell), T1059 (Command and Scripting Interpreter), T1548.003 (Sudo/Sudo Caching), T1053.003 (Cron), T1074.001 (Local Data Staging), T1070.006 (Timestomp), and T1048 (Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol).

challenges.name: [Alpha] HTTP 500 Error Count
challenges.description:
How many HTTP 500 (Internal Server Error) responses were recorded in the web-srv alerts during the attack?

HTTP 500 errors often indicate failed exploitation attempts or server-side crashes caused by the attacker's payloads. Count the total number of these error responses.

hints.content: Search for alerts on web-srv where the HTTP response status code is 500. This can be found in the full_log field of Apache access log alerts or in the data.http_status field.
In Apache access log format, the status code appears after the requested URL. You can search full_log for '" 500 ' (with surrounding spaces/quotes) to match the HTTP status code precisely.
The answer is a two-digit number between 40 and 50. These 500 errors were triggered by malformed SQLi payloads and resource-intensive scanning requests.

challenges.name: [Fenrir] FIM Evasion Technique
challenges.description:
The attacker attempted to evade Wazuh's File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) detection. What specific evasion technique did they use, and which Wazuh configuration file/directive should be modified to detect it?

Provide the answer in the format: technique_name:ossec_config_directive

Analyze the gaps in FIM detection and identify how the attacker tried to avoid triggering syscheck alerts.

hints.content: The attacker manipulated file timestamps (mtime) to make the web shell appear to have been created at the same time as legitimate files. This technique is called timestomping (T1070.006).

Wazuh's syscheck in default mode (scheduled scans) relies on comparing file attributes including timestamps. Switching to 'whodata' mode (real-time + audit) would detect changes regardless of timestamp manipulation.

The answer format is: timestomping:/var/ossec/etc/ossec.conf:whodata. The whodata directive in the syscheck configuration enables kernel-level audit monitoring that cannot be evaded by timestamp manipulation.

challenges.name: [Fenrir] Complete Attack Timeline
challenges.description:
Reconstruct the complete incident timeline. What are the UTC timestamps of the first reconnaissance event and the final exfiltration event?

Provide the answer as the two timestamps separated by a pipe: first_event|last_event

You must correlate events across multiple rule groups (web attacks, FIM, command execution, network) to identify the true first and last events of this intrusion.

hints.content: The reconnaissance started in the early morning (UTC). Search for the very first Nikto scan alert from the attacker's IP 198.51.100.23. Sort all alerts from this IP by timestamp ascending.
The exfiltration happened in the evening (UTC). Look for outbound network connection alerts or the last command execution event associated with the data staging/transfer after the mysqldump.
First event: Nikto scan begins at 08:14:23 UTC. Last event: curl-based exfiltration to external server completes at 19:47:51 UTC. Use the exact timestamps from the @timestamp field.

challenges.name: [Fenrir] Timestomped Files
challenges.description:
The attacker used timestomping (MITRE T1070.006) to alter file modification times and evade FIM detection. Identify ALL files on web-srv that had their timestamps manipulated by the attacker.

Provide the full paths of all timestomped files, separated by a pipe character (|), in the order they were modified.

hints.content: Look for alerts related to MITRE technique T1070.006 (Indicator Removal on Host: Timestomp) on web-srv. Also search for 'touch' command execution events in the audit logs, as 'touch' is commonly used to manipulate timestamps.

The attacker timestomped two files: one is the uploaded web shell, and the other is a critical Wazuh configuration file. Think about what the attacker would want to hide and what system files they might want to tamper with.
The two files are: the web shell at /var/www/html/dvwa/hackable/uploads/cmd.php (to hide its creation time) and /var/ossec/etc/ossec.conf (to conceal tampering with the Wazuh agent configuration).

challenges.name: [Fenrir] Base64 Staging Command
challenges.description:
Before splitting the exfiltration data into chunks, the attacker encoded the compressed archive using Base64 to allow safe transfer over HTTP. What is the exact base64 encoding command the attacker executed on web-srv?

Analyze the command execution audit trail between the tar compression and the split/exfiltration steps.

hints.content: Look for command execution alerts on web-srv that contain 'base64' in the command line. This event occurs chronologically between the tar compression and the split operation during the data staging phase.

The attacker used the standard Linux 'base64' command (not python or perl encoding). The command reads the compressed tar.gz file and redirects the output to a .b64 file in the same hidden staging directory.
The command format is: base64 <input_file> > <output_file>. Both files are in /tmp/.cache/. The input is loot.tar.gz and the output is loot.b64.

challenges.name: [Fenrir] Exfiltration Chunk Command
challenges.description:
The attacker split the Base64-encoded data into smaller chunks before exfiltrating via HTTP POST requests. What is the exact split command used to divide the encoded file into chunks?

Examine the command execution audit trail on web-srv for the data splitting operation that preceded the curl-based exfiltration.

hints.content: Search for command execution alerts containing 'split' on web-srv. The split command was used to divide the Base64 file into smaller chunks that could be exfiltrated individually via HTTP POST.
The 'split' command uses the -b flag to specify the chunk size in bytes/kilobytes. Look for a split command that references the .b64 file in the /tmp/.cache/ staging directory.
The exact format is: split -b 512k <input_file> <output_prefix>. The input is the .b64 file and the output prefix includes 'chunk_' in the same hidden directory.

challenges.name: [Fenrir] Complete Correlation Rule Sequence
challenges.description:
The WazuhBOTS custom ruleset includes a chain of correlation rules that progressively detect more complete attack patterns. List ALL custom correlation rule IDs (both scenario-specific 87900+ and cross-scenario 88000+) that fired during the Dark Harvest attack, in ascending order.

Provide the rule IDs separated by pipe characters (|).

hints.content: Search for all alerts on web-srv where rule.id >= 87900. These are custom WazuhBOTS rules. Sort by rule.id to see the complete set of correlation rules that triggered.

There are two sets of custom rules: scenario-specific rules (87900+) and cross-scenario correlation rules (88000+). The chain includes 87901 (web attack chain), 88000 (exploitation detected), 88001 (privesc + persistence), and 88002 (full kill chain with exfiltration).

The four rules form a detection hierarchy: 87901 detects SQLi-to-webshell chain, 88000 detects multi-phase exploitation, 88001 adds privilege escalation and persistence, 88002 represents full kill chain including exfiltration (level 15).

challenges.name: [Fenrir] Exfiltration URL Base
challenges.description:
The attacker exfiltrated data by sending chunked HTTP POST requests to an external server controlled by the attacker. What is the base URL (protocol, IP, and port) of the exfiltration endpoint?

Analyze the curl commands in the command execution audit trail or network connection alerts to determine the full base URL the attacker used for data exfiltration.

hints.content: Look for curl commands in the audit or command execution alerts on web-srv. The attacker used curl to POST data chunks to an external HTTP server. The base URL includes the attacker's IP and a non-standard port.
The exfiltration used plain HTTP (not HTTPS) to the attacker's known IP address 198.51.100.23 on the common C2 port. The URL format is http://IP:PORT.
Combine the attacker IP (198.51.100.23) with the exfiltration port (4444) in URL format: http://198.51.100.23:4444. The actual upload paths were /upload/data/0 through /data/15.
